Defendant Crane Co.'S Motion For Summary Judgment Or, In The Alternative, Summary Adjudication (Added To Calendar For Issuance Of Tentative Ruling Only)
Off Calendar Feb-09-2017 Off Calendar

MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Or, In The Alternative, Summary Adjudication
Off Calendar Feb-09-2017 Denied
MINI MINUTES:
APPEARANCES:
James P. Nevin of BRAYTON & PURCELL LLP (415-898-1555), appearing for plaintiffs / opposing parties Anthony Lloyd & Katherine Lloyd

RULING:
DEFENDANT CRANE CO.'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT OR, IN THE ALTERNATIVE, SUMMARY ADJUDICATION

No other appearance and tentative ruling adopted: Defendant Crane Co.' motion for summary judgment and alternate motion for summary adjudication are denied. As to summary judgment and summary adjudication issues nos. 1-4, defendant failed to sustain its burden of demonstrating that plaintiffs do not possess and cannot reasonably obtain evidence that Mr. Lloyd was exposed to asbestos-containing products or materials attributable to defendant and that such exposure falls outside the waiver stated in plaintiffs' complaint. Mr. Lloyd's deposition testimony (as to the bonnet gaskets removed from, and installed onto, Crane valves) and plaintiffs' responses to defendant's special interrogatories preclude defendant from successfully invoking the factually devoid prong of Aguilar v. Atlantic Richfield Co. (2001) 25 Cal.4th 826. Even if defendant did sustain its initial burden, Mr. Lloyd's deposition testimony and declaration and the testimony of William McLean and Anthony Pantaleoni (Exhibits I, J, K) create a triable issue whether Mr. Lloyd was exposed to asbestos-containing products or materials (gaskets) attributable to defendant. Defendant failed to sustain its burden of demonstrating that plaintiffs do not possess and cannot reasonably obtain evidence that exposure to asbestos-containing products or materials attributable to defendant was a substantial factor in causing or contributing to Mr. Lloyd's increased risk of developing his asbestos-related illness. Plaintiffs' responses to defendant's special discovery preclude defendant from successfully invoking the factually devoid prong of Aguilar. As to summary adjudication issue no. 5 (punitive damages), defendant failed to sustain its burden of demonstrating that plaintiffs do not possess, and cannot reasonably obtain, clear and convincing evidence that defendant acted with the requisite malice, fraud or oppression to warrant the imposition of punitive damages. Plaintiffs' responses to defendant's special interrogatories preclude defendant from successfully invoking the factually devoid prong of Aguilar. Even if defendant sustained its initial burden, plaintiffs' evidence (Exhibits G, I, K, L, R, T, BB) creates a triable issue whether defendant acted with the requisite malice, fraud or oppression to warrant the imposition of punitive damages. While the Court takes the higher proof standard into consideration, plaintiffs do not need to prove their case for punitive damages at the summary adjudication stage and the Court cannot conclude that no reasonable jury could find plaintiffs' evidence to be clear and convincing proof of malice, fraud or oppression. (See American Airlines, Inc. v. Sheppard, Mullin, Richter & Hampton (2002) 96 Cal.App.4th 1017; Hoch v. Allied-Signal, Inc. (1994) 24 Cal.App.4th 48.) Order signed in open court. Judge: Cynthia Ming-mei Lee, matter not reported (503/CML)
